MKT10009 Marketing and the Consumer ExperienceLecture 11
Chapter 14 from our Textbook: Principles of Marketing,
8th edition
Presented by Dr Gordon Campbell

Learning Objectives for Lecture 11 Part 4
14.4 Describe the principles of sustainable marketing.

Part 4 – Business actions towards sustainable marketing

Business actions towards sustainable marketing (1 of 5)
Sustainable marketing principles
A company’s marketing should be guided by five sustainable marketing principles:
consumer-oriented marketing
customer-value marketing
innovative marketing
sense-of-mission marketing
societal marketing.

Business actions towards sustainable marketing (2 of 5)
Sustainable marketing principles (cont.)
Consumer-oriented marketing is the philosophy of sustainable marketing which holds that the company should consider and organise its marketing activities from the consumer’s point of view.
Customer-value marketing is a principle of sustainable marketing which holds that a company should put most of its resources into customer-value-building marketing investments.

Business actions towards sustainable marketing (3 of 5)
Sustainable marketing principles (cont.)
Innovative marketing is a principle of sustainable marketing which requires that a company seek real product and marketing improvements.
Sense-of-mission marketing is a principle of sustainable marketing which holds that a company should define its mission in broad social terms rather than in narrow product terms.
Q: ‘Brands linked with broader missions can serve the best long-run interests of both the brand and consumer.’ Do you agree? Why, or why not?

Business actions towards sustainable marketing (4 of 5)
Sustainable marketing principles (cont.)
Societal marketing is the idea that a company’s marketing decisions should consider the consumers’ wants, the company’s requirements, consumers’ long-run interests and society’s long-run interests.
Alert companies view societal problems as opportunities.
Sustainable marketing calls for products that are not only pleasing but also provide long-run consumer benefits.
Q: Pleasing products give high immediate satisfaction but may hurt consumers in the long run. Can you think of some examples?

Figure 14.4
